summary: The post examines a controversy at Columbia where a machine learning assignment about Stop and Frisk data was poorly framed, sparking student protests. O'Neil argues this incident highlights the urgent need for CS departments to teach ethics alongside technical skills. She then explores a Stanford paper proposing algorithms to detect and reduce racist policing practices, while expressing skepticism about real-time algorithmic interventions. She suggests precinct-level analysis of hit rates might be more effective than equipping individual officers with algorithms, concluding that while data can help combat racist practices, the implementation details remain unclear.
key_points: CS departments have an obligation to teach ethics of machine learning alongside mathematical techniques
Machine learning algorithms are not objective because they're trained on historical data reflecting racist practices
Stop and Frisk data shows lower 'hit rates' for finding weapons on Black people than white people, even in high-crime areas, suggesting racial bias
Real-time algorithms for individual officers risk gaming and evasion; precinct-level analysis may be more effective
The concept of 'reasonable suspicion' is fundamentally a statistical construction that judges have applied inconsistently
money_quotes: Machine learning algorithms are not objective, because the data going into them are historical artifacts of racist police practices.

I've been saying for years that the CS department at Columbia, like many CS departments everywhere, has an obligation to teach and think about the ethics of machine learning as well as the mathematical techniques.
One of the best things about this article is the historical context it gives about the extent to which 'reasonable suspicion' is a statistical construction.
There are obvious issues around gaming such a model, or otherwise learning to evade undesired outcomes.
Although I am excited about the idea of using data to track and help prevent racist practices, I don't think we know exactly what that would look like in practice.
key_insight: Data analysis could help identify and reduce racist policing patterns, but implementation at the systemic level is more promising than giving individual officers real-time algorithmic tools that could be gamed.
